claim.asCirculated: "Hinduism literally has 330 million gods" — used both as mockery ('how do you even keep track') and as boast ('we have the most gods').

whatsTrue: The phrase trayastriṃśat koṭi ('33 koṭi') is genuinely Vedic-adjacent, and later tradition itself popularly speaks of '33 crore devas' — the number circulates inside the tradition too.
whatsDistorted: The Vedic base count is thirty-three devas — Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 3.9.1 stages exactly this exchange: asked how many gods there are, Yājñavalkya walks the answer down from 'three thousand three hundred and six' to thirty-three (8 Vasus, 11 Rudras, 12 Ādityas, Indra, Prajāpati) and ultimately to one, brahman. Koṭi means both 'crore (10⁷)' and 'class/kind/pinnacle' — '33 koṭi' read as '33 classes of deity' matches the enumerated 33; read as arithmetic it becomes 330 million. The mistranslation freezes the arithmetic reading and deletes the passage's own punchline: the many resolve into one.

supporting.point: '33 crore' is used devotionally/idiomatically within the living tradition; the number is not purely an external invention.

sources.label: Monier-Williams, Sanskrit-English Dictionary, s.v. koṭi — both senses attested

countering.point: The canonical enumeration is 33, and the source text itself reduces even that toward one — the opposite of the 'endless literal pantheon' framing.

sources.label: Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 3.9.1 (Olivelle, The Early Upaniṣads, OUP 1998)
Macdonell, A.A., Vedic Mythology (1897) — the 33-deva scheme

verdict.rationale: A polysemous term rendered by its only sensational sense, contradicting the cited text's own resolution. When used as an internal boast, file the same facts under category 9 — the ledger's rule is symmetric.

sources.label: Olivelle, P., The Early Upaniṣads (1998) — BU 3.9.1

quickRebuttal: The Vedic count is thirty-three devas — and the source text itself does the debunking: Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 3.9.1 walks the answer down from 3,306 to 33 to ONE. 'Koṭi' means 'class/kind' as well as 'crore'; '330 million literal gods' freezes the arithmetic reading and deletes the punchline.

spread.note: Circulates as both mockery and boast — the ledger's symmetry rule applies.
knownVariants: How do Hindus even keep track of 330 million gods?
